title: run.oci.keep_original_groups=1 doesn't always work with 'exec'
body:
/kind bug

**Description**

I am trying to use the `run.oci.keep_original_groups=1` annotation to let users [access the host's Docker socket](https://github.com/containers/toolbox/issues/430) via membership in the `docker` group on the host.

So, if you have:
```
$ ls -l /run/docker.sock 
srw-rw----. 1 root docker 0 Aug 19 15:44 /run/docker.sock
$ id
uid=1000(rishi) gid=1000(rishi) groups=1000(rishi),10(wheel),973(docker) context=unconfined_u:unconfined_r:unconfined_t:s0-s0:c0.c1023
```

... then this works:
```
$ podman run --annotation run.oci.keep_original_groups=1 --env TERM=$TERM -it --rm --security-opt label=disable --volume /run:/run/host/run:rslave registry.fedoraproject.org/f32/fedora-toolbox:32 /bin/bash
# sudo dnf -y install moby-engine
...
...
# docker --host unix:///run/host/run/docker.sock version
...
...
```

However, Toolbox is a bit weird, and `run.oci.keep_original_groups=1` doesn't seem to have the intended effect. It uses `exec` to spawn an interactive shell, uses `--userns=keep-id` and `useradd --uid ...` to create a user inside the container that matches the host.

The steps are somewhat as follows ...

Create a container and start it:
```
$ podman create --annotation run.oci.keep_original_groups=1 --name foo --security-opt label=disable --user root:root --userns=keep-id --volume /run:/run/host/run:rslave registry.fedoraproject.org/f32/fedora-toolbox:32 sleep +Inf
...
$ podman start foo
```

Create a matching user inside the container:
```
$ podman exec --env TERM=$TERM --interactive --tty --user root:root foo /bin/bash
# useradd --home-dir /home/rishi --no-create-home --shell /bin/bash --uid 1000 --groups wheel rishi
# passwd --delete rishi
# passwd --delete root
```

Enter the container and try to use Docker:
```
$ podman exec --env TERM=$TERM --interactive --tty --user rishi foo /bin/bash
$ sudo dnf -y install moby-engine
...
...
$ docker --host unix:///run/host/run/docker.sock version
...
Got permission denied while trying to connect to the Docker daemon socket at unix:///run/host/run/docker.sock: Get "http://%2Frun%2Fhost%2Frun%2Fdocker.sock/v1.40/version": dial unix /run/host/run/docker.sock: connect: permission denied
```

Notice how it runs into a permission error trying to access the Docker daemon over the socket.


**Output of `podman version`:**

```
Version:      2.0.3
API Version:  1
Go Version:   go1.13.11
Built:        Thu Jan  1 01:00:00 1970
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

comments.body: @giuseppe Poke - is the preserve groups annotation preserved in exec sessions? Should we maybe make it a separate option for exec?

> @debarshiray can you do `$ grep ^Groups /proc/$PID_EXEC_SHELL/status`.
> What does it show?

With `--user rishi`, I get:
```
$ grep ^Groups /proc/$$/status
Groups:	10
```

The entire file:

what happens if you don't specify `--user rishi` ?

# grep ^Groups /proc/$$/status

```
# grep ^Groups /proc/$$/status

```

your version of crun overwrites the groups when they are explicitely set.

It was fixed with 79f853cf119369b710346c5949d8dd45be30ad7c and in 0.14 so that keep_original_groups is always honored

> your version of crun overwrites the groups when they are explicitely set.
> 
> It was fixed with 79f853cf119369b710346c5949d8dd45be30ad7c and
> in 0.14 so that keep_original_groups is always honored

Oh, you are right. I was missing some updates and had `crun-0.13`. Sorry for wasting your time. :(

By the way, what did you mean by *when they are explicitly set*? Just trying to understand the situation a bit better for the future.
that the conf file has a `user` setting.  In your case it has since you specify `--user` so it overrides the groups you originally had
